claimText: Superbugs are strains of bacteria, viruses, parasites, and fungi that have evolved to become resistant to most antimicrobials.
evidenceExcerpt: Strains of bacteria, viruses, parasites, and fungi that are resistant to most antimicrobials are called Superbugs.
examUse: Statement-based question identifying the scope of pathogens classified as Superbugs.
distractorHooks: Superbugs refer exclusively to antibiotic-resistant bacteria
Superbugs are synthetic pathogens created in laboratories

claimText: Antimicrobial resistance spreads among microbes through both vertical gene transfer during division and horizontal gene transfer via the exchange of resistance genes.
evidenceExcerpt: Spread through vertical gene transfer during bacterial division and through horizontal gene transfer by exchange of resistance genes among microbes.
examUse: Statement-based question on the genetic mechanisms of AMR transmission.
distractorHooks: AMR spreads exclusively through vertical gene transfer
Horizontal gene transfer does not occur in antimicrobial resistance

claimText: The use of antimicrobials as growth promoters in intensive farming and the livestock sector is a key driver of Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR).
evidenceExcerpt: Key Drivers of AMR: ... Agriculture & Allied Sector (Use of antimicrobials in intensive farming and the Livestock sector used as growth-promoters)
examUse: Multiple-statement question identifying anthropogenic causes of AMR.
distractorHooks: Antimicrobials used in livestock do not affect human AMR
AMR is solely driven by human pharmaceutical consumption

claimText: Industrial waste generated from the production of Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients (APIs) is a key driver of Antimicrobial Resistance.
evidenceExcerpt: Key Drivers of AMR: ... Pharmaceutical Manufacturing (Industrial waste from the production of Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients)
examUse: Statement-based question on environmental and industrial factors contributing to AMR.
distractorHooks: API manufacturing waste is sterile and unrelated to AMR
Only biological waste from healthcare facilities causes AMR

claimText: The World Health Organization (WHO) recognizes Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) as one of the top ten global health threats, often describing it as a silent pandemic.
evidenceExcerpt: WHO recognized AMR as one of the top ten global health threats. It is often described as a silent pandemic.
examUse: Factual statement in a multi-statement question about global health initiatives and WHO classifications.
distractorHooks: WHO recently removed AMR from its top ten global health threats
AMR is officially classified by WHO as a localized epidemic rather than a pandemic threat
